Trevor Brown · Portfolio Projects
Home Use Cases Games Projects

Portfolio Projects

Four production-quality React applications built for broadcast journalism and data-driven storytelling. Each uses Next.js 15, React Server Components, and modern patterns that demonstrate real-world engineering competence.

Next.js 15 React Server Components TypeScript Journalism-focused
Project 01

Newsroom Dashboard (Next.js 15)

A real-time newsroom operations dashboard tracking story assignments, publication pipeline, traffic analytics, and social engagement. Built as a full-stack Next.js 15 app with Server Components for data-heavy views and Client Components for interactive controls.

Next.js 15 App Router React Server Components Server Actions Suspense Boundaries WebSocket Prisma
Architecture
Next.js 15 App Router → Layout (RSC) → Page (RSC)
Suspense → StoryTable (RSC) LiveFeed (Client)
Server Actions → Prisma ORM → PostgreSQL

How I Built This

  • App Router with nested layouts: /dashboard/stories, /dashboard/analytics, /dashboard/team each with their own loading states
  • Server Components fetch story data directly from the database via Prisma, sending zero JavaScript for data-heavy tables
  • Client-side LiveFeed component uses WebSocket for real-time story updates, auto-reconnecting on disconnect
  • Server Actions handle story status updates (draft, review, published) with optimistic UI via useOptimistic
  • Suspense boundaries wrap each dashboard section independently, so analytics can load without blocking the story list
  • Role-based access using Next.js middleware: editors see assignment controls, reporters see their stories only

What This Demonstrates

RSC Data Fetching Server Actions Streaming SSR WebSocket Integration Auth Middleware Prisma ORM
Project 02

Election Night Live Tracker

A broadcast-quality election night results page with live vote counting, interactive maps, projected winner calls, and Electoral College tracking. Styled to match the on-screen graphics of network election coverage.

React + D3 Hybrid Server-Sent Events Framer Motion GeoJSON Maps useSyncExternalStore
Real-time Data Flow
SSE Endpoint → External Store → useSyncExternalStore
Race Components → StateMap (D3 geo) VoteBars (Motion)
EC Counter Call Overlay Ticker Tape

How I Built This

  • Server-Sent Events stream vote updates from a Node.js backend, with useSyncExternalStore bridging the event source to React state
  • D3.js renders the geographic map (geoAlbersUsa projection) while React manages the component tree and state -- a hybrid rendering approach
  • Framer Motion animates vote count bars with spring physics, creating the dramatic "counting" effect seen on broadcast networks
  • "Projected Winner" callouts trigger when a candidate crosses the victory threshold, with configurable margins for competitive vs. safe states
  • Electoral College counter aggregates called states in real-time, animating the needle toward 270 with Framer Motion's layout animations
  • Ticker tape at the bottom scrolls latest race calls using CSS animation, matching the broadcast TV lower-third aesthetic

What This Demonstrates

React + D3 Integration Real-time Streaming Animation External Store Pattern Broadcast Design GeoJSON
Project 03

Data Journalism CMS

A content management system built for data journalism teams. Reporters upload datasets (CSV, JSON), create interactive chart embeds, and publish stories with inline visualizations. Editors review and approve before publication.

Next.js 15 TipTap Rich Editor Chart Builder File Upload RBAC Vercel Blob
CMS Architecture
TipTap Editor (Client) → Chart Embed Block → D3 Preview
Dataset Upload → Vercel Blob Storage → Parser (CSV/JSON)
Publish Pipeline → Review Queue → Public Article (ISR)

How I Built This

  • TipTap rich text editor with custom extension for embedding interactive charts -- reporters drag datasets into the editor
  • Chart builder component lets reporters pick chart type (bar, line, scatter, map), map columns to axes, and customize colors
  • Dataset files upload to Vercel Blob via Server Actions, with progress tracking and schema auto-detection from CSV headers
  • Role-based access: reporters create/edit, editors approve, admins manage users. Implemented with Next.js middleware and JWT claims
  • Published articles use ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) for fast loading, with on-demand revalidation when edits are made
  • Preview mode lets editors see the full published layout before approving, using Next.js Draft Mode

What This Demonstrates

Rich Text Editor File Upload Data Visualization RBAC ISR CMS Architecture
Project 04

Interactive Story Builder

A scrollytelling framework for creating immersive data-driven stories. Journalists design scroll-triggered animations, chart transitions, and media sequences without writing code. Think Scrollama meets a visual editor.

Scrollytelling IntersectionObserver React Spring Drag-and-Drop Story Format Accessibility
Story Builder Architecture
Visual Editor → Story JSON → Renderer
Scroll Triggers → IntersectionObserver → Animation Queue
Media Blocks Chart Blocks Text Blocks Map Blocks

How I Built This

  • Visual editor uses drag-and-drop (dnd-kit) to arrange story blocks: text, chart, image, video, and map sections in a vertical sequence
  • Scroll triggers powered by IntersectionObserver with configurable thresholds (enter at 25%, 50%, 75%) for precise timing
  • React Spring handles scroll-linked animations: fade-in, parallax, chart transitions, and element reveals with spring physics
  • Story format is a JSON document describing the block sequence, animation configs, and data sources -- portable and versionable
  • Accessibility: all scroll-triggered content has static fallbacks, reduced-motion media query support, and keyboard navigation
  • Export to static HTML: the renderer can output a standalone page with embedded data, no server required for published stories

What This Demonstrates

Scrollytelling Drag and Drop Scroll-linked Animation Visual Editor Accessibility Static Export
